---
title: "Conditional Probability, Independence, and Bayes' Rule"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Probability Foundations"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

POINT  = "#2b3a67"     # data / samples
LINE   = "#c1121f"     # the truth / theoretical value
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights
GREY   = "#6c757d"
SETA   = "#3a7ca5"     # event A
SETB   = "#d98b2b"     # event B
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the second article of the **Probability
> Foundations** track. It assumes only [PROB-00 *What Is Probability? Sample Spaces and the
> Axioms*] — the sample space $\Omega$, events as subsets, and Kolmogorov's three axioms
> (non-negativity, $P(\Omega)=1$, countable additivity), from which we derived the
> complement rule, monotonicity, and inclusion–exclusion. Nothing above the Calculus-I
> floor is assumed. This article answers the next question every later article needs: **how
> does a probability change when we learn that something happened, and how do we invert that
> relationship?** It builds **conditional probability**, **independence**, the **law of
> total probability**, and **Bayes' rule**. It sets up [PROB-08 *Independence and the i.i.d.
> Sample*] (independence lifted from events to random variables), [PROB-09 *Conditional
> Distributions and Conditional Expectation*] (conditioning at the random-variable level),
> and [PROB-12 *The Poisson Distribution*] (built on independence). Cross-track, naive-Bayes
> classification, Bayesian inference, and causal reasoning all consume what we build here.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Probability as built in [PROB-00] answers questions about an experiment *before* it is run.
But information arrives: a test comes back positive, a card is revealed, a sensor fires.
**Conditional probability** is the machine for updating a probability in light of what we
have learned, and it is the single most leveraged idea in applied probability. This article
constructs it from one picture — **conditioning is restricting the sample space to the
event we now know occurred, and renormalizing so the new, smaller world again has total
mass one.** From that definition we derive the **multiplication rule** and the **chain
rule**, define **independence** as the precise statement "learning $B$ tells us nothing
about $A$," and settle the perennial confusion between *independent* and *mutually
exclusive* — which, far from being the same thing, are nearly opposite. We then partition
the sample space and derive the **law of total probability**, and from it **Bayes' rule**,
the formula that inverts a conditional: it turns $P(\text{evidence}\mid\text{cause})$, which
we can measure, into $P(\text{cause}\mid\text{evidence})$, which we actually want. We carry
it through the medical-test example every interview asks for — where a 99%-accurate test for
a rare disease is *wrong most of the time it says "positive"* — and through spam filtering,
where independence is assumed even though it is false, on purpose. The single claim this
article defends: **Bayes' rule is not a trick but the only consistent way to reason backward
from evidence to cause, and the base rate is the term everyone forgets.**

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
To **condition** on an event $B$ (one we have learned occurred) is to **shrink the sample
space down to $B$** and rescale every probability so that the new world $B$ has total mass
$1$ again. That single move is the definition
$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}, \qquad P(B) > 0 .
$$
Everything else falls out of it. Rearranged, it is the **multiplication rule**
$P(A \cap B) = P(A\mid B)\,P(B)$. Two events are **independent** when conditioning changes
nothing — $P(A\mid B) = P(A)$, equivalently $P(A\cap B)=P(A)P(B)$. And reading the
multiplication rule from both sides at once and dividing gives **Bayes' rule**,
$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A)\,P(A)}{P(B)},
$$
the formula that flips a conditional probability around so we can reason from evidence back
to its cause.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The definition looks like a one-line fraction, but the
*why* behind it — that conditioning is literally a change of the universe we are measuring
in — is what makes Bayes' rule feel inevitable rather than memorized, and what explains the
single most common error in all of applied probability: confusing $P(A\mid B)$ with
$P(B\mid A)$.

# Why we are doing this: the problem of learning {#sec-why}

Hold one question in view for the whole article: **probability quantifies uncertainty, but
uncertainty is not static — we learn things. What is the rule for updating?** Every move
below answers a piece of that.

## The problem the axioms left open

[PROB-00] gave us a fixed probability $P(A)$ for each event, computed before the experiment
runs. That is enough to ask "what is the chance this fair die shows a six?" — answer $1/6$,
settled before the roll. But almost no real question is static. A doctor does not ask "what
is the chance a random person has this disease?"; she asks "what is the chance *this patient*
has it, **given** that their test came back positive?" A spam filter does not ask "what
fraction of all email is spam?"; it asks "what is the chance *this* message is spam, **given**
that it contains the word *invoice* and a link?" The defining feature of every one of these
is the word **given**: a piece of information has arrived, and it should move the
probability.

The pre-conditioning machinery has no slot for "given." It computes $P(A)$ and stops. We
need a second operation that takes the old probability and a piece of news, and returns the
*updated* probability. That operation is conditioning, and the entire enterprise of learning
from data — inference, classification, Bayesian updating, the lot — is built on it.

## Why the historical thread runs through Bayes

The forward direction is usually easy to measure and the backward direction is what we want.
A lab can tell you $P(\text{test positive}\mid\text{diseased})$ — give the test to known-sick
patients and count. A botanist can tell you $P(\text{this leaf shape}\mid\text{species }A)$ —
measure many known specimens. But the patient and the field biologist face the *inverse*: a
positive test, a leaf in hand — *which cause produced this evidence?* That is
$P(\text{diseased}\mid\text{positive})$, $P(\text{species }A\mid\text{this leaf})$ — the
backward conditional.

The Reverend **Thomas Bayes**, in an essay published posthumously in 1763 by his friend
Richard Price, asked exactly this: given that we have observed an effect, how do we reason
back to the probability of its cause? **Pierre-Simon Laplace**, working independently around
1774 and far more generally, turned it into the engine of an entire approach to probability
and statistics. The formula they arrived at — Bayes' rule — is the *only* consistent way to
invert a conditional, and we will derive it from nothing but the definition of conditioning.
That is the *why* we hold onto: conditioning lets us learn, and Bayes' rule is what lets us
learn in the direction reality forces on us — backward, from evidence to cause.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why not just define $P(A\mid B)$ directly, instead of as a ratio?"* Because the ratio is
forced, not chosen — it is the *only* definition that keeps conditioning consistent with the
[PROB-00] axioms (we show in @sec-defn that $P(\cdot\mid B)$ is itself a genuine probability,
and the ratio is what makes it so) and that matches the frequentist meaning "among the runs
where $B$ happened, what fraction also had $A$." Define it any other way and either it stops
obeying the axioms or it stops matching long-run frequencies. The interviewer is checking
whether you see conditioning as a *derived, forced* construction, not a fresh axiom.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Carry forward the one picture from [PROB-00]: **probability is normalized area**, with the
whole sample space $\Omega$ a region of total area $1$ and each event a sub-region whose area
*is* its probability. Conditioning has an equally simple picture on top of that.

**To condition on $B$ is to throw away everything outside $B$ and zoom in.** We have learned
$B$ occurred, so outcomes outside $B$ are now impossible — erase them. What remains is the
region $B$, and the only part of $A$ that can still happen is the part inside $B$, namely
$A \cap B$. The updated probability of $A$ should be *the fraction of the new world $B$ that
is taken up by $A$* — that is, the area of $A\cap B$ divided by the area of $B$:
$$
P(A\mid B) = \frac{\text{area of } A\cap B}{\text{area of } B}.
$$
The division by area-of-$B$ is a **renormalization**: $B$ was only part of the original unit
square, with area $P(B) < 1$, but now $B$ *is* the whole world and must have total
probability $1$ again. Dividing by $P(B)$ rescales it back up to $1$. @fig-condition draws
exactly this zoom.

```{python}
#| label: fig-condition
#| fig-cap: 'Conditioning as restriction and renormalization. **Left:** the original sample space $\\Omega$ (unit square, area $1$) with events $A$ (blue) and $B$ (orange). The probability of $A$ is its area. **Right:** we learn $B$ occurred, so $B$ becomes the entire world — everything outside it is erased. The only surviving part of $A$ is $A\\cap B$ (purple). The conditional probability $P(A\\mid B)$ is the fraction of $B$''s area occupied by $A\\cap B$; dividing by $P(B)$ renormalizes the shrunken world back to total mass $1$.'
from matplotlib.patches import Circle, Rectangle
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 4.2))

# Left: full sample space with A and B overlapping
axL.add_patch(Rectangle((0, 0), 1, 1, fill=False, edgecolor="black", lw=1.5))
cA = Circle((0.42, 0.55), 0.27, color=SETA, alpha=0.45)
cB = Circle((0.64, 0.45), 0.30, color=SETB, alpha=0.45)
axL.add_patch(cA); axL.add_patch(cB)
axL.text(0.27, 0.62, "$A$", fontsize=13, color=SETA)
axL.text(0.84, 0.40, "$B$", fontsize=13, color=SETB)
axL.text(0.55, 0.50, r"$A\cap B$", fontsize=8, ha="center")
axL.text(0.5, 1.07, r"$\Omega$ (area $=1$)", ha="center", fontsize=12)
axL.set_xlim(-0.05, 1.05); axL.set_ylim(-0.05, 1.18)
axL.set_aspect("equal"); axL.axis("off")
axL.set_title("Before: $P(A)$ is area of $A$", fontsize=11)

# Right: zoom into B, the new universe
axR.add_patch(Circle((0.5, 0.5), 0.40, color=SETB, alpha=0.30,
                      edgecolor=SETB, lw=1.8))
# A cap B as a lens inside B
axR.add_patch(Circle((0.36, 0.58), 0.22, color="#7b4fa0", alpha=0.55))
axR.text(0.5, 0.18, "$B$ is now the whole world", ha="center", fontsize=10, color=SETB)
axR.text(0.34, 0.60, r"$A\cap B$", ha="center", fontsize=9, color="white")
axR.text(0.5, 1.02, r"After: $P(A\mid B)=\dfrac{P(A\cap B)}{P(B)}$",
         ha="center", fontsize=12)
axR.set_xlim(-0.05, 1.05); axR.set_ylim(-0.05, 1.18)
axR.set_aspect("equal"); axR.axis("off")
axR.set_title("After: renormalize $B$ to mass $1$", fontsize=11)

plt.tight_layout()
```

If that picture is solid, the rest of the article is bookkeeping on it. Independence is "the
fraction of $B$ taken by $A$ equals the fraction of all of $\Omega$ taken by $A$ — zooming
into $B$ changed nothing." Bayes' rule is "I can measure the fraction one way but need it the
other way, so I flip the ratio using the multiplication rule." Keep the zoom in mind.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

We proceed in dependency order: the definition and its first consequence (it is a genuine
probability), then the multiplication and chain rules, independence, the law of total
probability, and finally Bayes' rule. Every step rests only on the [PROB-00] axioms and the
results derived there.

## The definition, and why it is forced {#sec-defn}

::: {#def-conditional}
**Definition (conditional probability).** Let $A$ and $B$ be events with $P(B) > 0$. The
**conditional probability of $A$ given $B$**, written $P(A\mid B)$ (read "$P$ of $A$ given
$B$"), is
$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} .
$$ {#eq-conditional}
The vertical bar $\mid$ is read "given"; it is *not* division — the division is the fraction
on the right. The requirement $P(B) > 0$ is essential: conditioning on something we assigned
zero probability would divide by zero, and "given an impossible event" has no meaning. (The
case $P(B)=0$ with continuous variables is handled differently and is built in [PROB-09]; here
$B$ always has positive probability.)
:::

The symbol $A\cap B$ is the intersection from [PROB-00]: the event that $A$ **and** $B$ both
occur. So @eq-conditional says: the updated chance of $A$ is the chance that *both* happened,
as a fraction of the chance that the thing we conditioned on happened.

Why is this fraction the *right* definition, and not merely *a* definition? Two reasons,
both promised in @sec-why.

**First — it is the only thing consistent with the axioms.** Once we declare that learning
$B$ makes $B$ the new sample space, the updated probability $Q(A) := P(A\mid B)$ must itself
obey Kolmogorov's three axioms ([PROB-00])— otherwise "updating" would produce an incoherent
probability. Let us verify that @eq-conditional delivers exactly that, treating $B$ as the
new universe. We check all three axioms.

- **(A1) Non-negativity.** $Q(A) = P(A\cap B)/P(B)$. The numerator $P(A\cap B)\ge 0$ by (A1)
  for $P$, and the denominator $P(B) > 0$ by assumption, so the ratio is $\ge 0$.
- **(A2) Normalization.** The new universe is $B$, so we need $Q(B) = 1$. Indeed
  $Q(B) = P(B\cap B)/P(B) = P(B)/P(B) = 1$, using $B\cap B = B$.
- **(A3) Countable additivity.** Let $A_1, A_2, \dots$ be pairwise-disjoint events. Then the
  events $A_1\cap B, A_2\cap B, \dots$ are *also* pairwise disjoint — if $A_i\cap A_j =
  \varnothing$ then certainly $(A_i\cap B)\cap(A_j\cap B)\subseteq A_i\cap A_j = \varnothing$.
  Also $\left(\bigcup_i A_i\right)\cap B = \bigcup_i (A_i\cap B)$ by the distributive law of
  sets (verified by outcome-chasing in [PROB-00]). So
  $$
  Q\!\left(\bigcup_i A_i\right)
  = \frac{P\!\left(\bigcup_i (A_i\cap B)\right)}{P(B)}
  = \frac{\sum_i P(A_i\cap B)}{P(B)}
  = \sum_i \frac{P(A_i\cap B)}{P(B)}
  = \sum_i Q(A_i),
  $$
  where the second equality is (A3) for the original $P$ applied to the disjoint events
  $A_i\cap B$, and the third just distributes the fixed divisor $P(B)$ across the sum.

So $Q(\cdot) = P(\cdot\mid B)$ is a genuine probability on the restricted world $B$. *That*
is the sense in which the ratio is forced: it is the unique rescaling of $P$ that makes $B$
into a legitimate sample space of its own. Any other formula would break one of the three
axioms.

**Second — it matches the frequentist meaning.** Imagine running the experiment $n$ times.
Let $n_B$ be the number of runs on which $B$ occurred, and $n_{A\cap B}$ the number on which
both $A$ and $B$ occurred. "Among the runs where $B$ happened, what fraction also had $A$?"
is $n_{A\cap B}/n_B$. Divide numerator and denominator by the total $n$:
$$
\frac{n_{A\cap B}}{n_B} = \frac{n_{A\cap B}/n}{n_B/n}
\;\xrightarrow[\;n\to\infty\;]{}\; \frac{P(A\cap B)}{P(B)},
$$
since each relative frequency tends to its probability (the long-run-frequency reading from
[PROB-00]; that the limits exist is the Law of Large Numbers, owned by the Inference track and
built on the Chebyshev inequality of [PROB-19]). The limit is exactly @eq-conditional. So the
ratio is not an arbitrary choice — it is *both* the unique axiom-consistent rescaling *and*
the long-run fraction the word "given" plainly means.

## The multiplication rule and the chain rule {#sec-mult}

Clearing the denominator in @eq-conditional gives a rule for the probability that two things
*both* happen — often far easier to reason about than $P(A\cap B)$ directly.

::: {#thm-mult}
**Theorem (multiplication rule).** For events with $P(B) > 0$,
$$
P(A \cap B) = P(A \mid B)\,P(B).
$$ {#eq-mult}
Symmetrically, if $P(A) > 0$, then $P(A\cap B) = P(B\mid A)\,P(A)$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Multiply both sides of the definition @eq-conditional, $P(A\mid B) = P(A\cap B)/P(B)$, by
$P(B)$. Since $P(B)>0$ we may do this without dividing by zero, and the $P(B)$ on the right
cancels: $P(A\mid B)\,P(B) = P(A\cap B)$. The symmetric form swaps the roles of $A$ and $B$
in @eq-conditional, which is legitimate because $A\cap B = B\cap A$ (intersection does not
care about order), and uses $P(A)>0$ instead. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The intuition: to have *both* $A$ and $B$, first $B$ must happen (probability $P(B)$), and
then, in that restricted world, $A$ must also happen (probability $P(A\mid B)$). Multiply the
two stages. This staging extends to any number of events, and the extension is the workhorse
behind sequential models, decision trees, and the likelihood of a data sequence. Rather than
assert it, we derive it.

::: {#thm-chain}
**Theorem (chain rule / general multiplication rule).** For events $A_1, \dots, A_n$ with
$P(A_1\cap\cdots\cap A_{n-1}) > 0$,
$$
P(A_1\cap A_2\cap\cdots\cap A_n)
= P(A_1)\,P(A_2\mid A_1)\,P(A_3\mid A_1\cap A_2)\cdots
  P(A_n\mid A_1\cap\cdots\cap A_{n-1}).
$$ {#eq-chain}
:::

::: {.proof}
We use **induction on $n$** — prove it for $n=2$, then show that if it holds for $n-1$ events
it holds for $n$, which together cover every $n\ge 2$.

*Base case $n=2$.* This is exactly the multiplication rule @eq-mult written as
$P(A_1\cap A_2) = P(A_1)\,P(A_2\mid A_1)$ (the symmetric form, with $A_1$ playing the role of
the conditioning event). Already proved.

*Inductive step.* Assume @eq-chain holds for any $n-1$ events. Write
$C = A_1\cap A_2\cap\cdots\cap A_{n-1}$ for the intersection of the first $n-1$. We are given
$P(C) > 0$. Apply the two-event multiplication rule @eq-mult to the pair $C$ and $A_n$,
treating $C$ as a single event:
$$
P(A_1\cap\cdots\cap A_n) = P(C \cap A_n) = P(A_n \mid C)\,P(C).
$$
Now $A_n\mid C$ is precisely $P(A_n\mid A_1\cap\cdots\cap A_{n-1})$ — the last factor of
@eq-chain. And $P(C) = P(A_1\cap\cdots\cap A_{n-1})$, to which the inductive hypothesis
applies (its conditioning intersection $P(A_1\cap\cdots\cap A_{n-2})\ge P(C) > 0$ by
monotonicity from [PROB-00], so the hypothesis is valid), expanding it into the first $n-1$
factors of @eq-chain:
$$
P(C) = P(A_1)\,P(A_2\mid A_1)\cdots P(A_{n-1}\mid A_1\cap\cdots\cap A_{n-2}).
$$
Substituting this for $P(C)$ above stacks the first $n-1$ factors in front of the last one,
giving exactly @eq-chain for $n$ events. By induction the formula holds for all
$n\ge 2$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

## Independence — when learning $B$ tells us nothing about $A$ {#sec-independence}

Sometimes the news that $B$ occurred leaves $A$'s probability completely unchanged. Knowing
the first coin came up heads tells you nothing about the second coin. That is **independence**,
and the cleanest way to define it is to say exactly that: conditioning does not move the
probability.

::: {#def-independence}
**Definition (independence of two events).** Events $A$ and $B$ are **independent** if
$$
P(A \cap B) = P(A)\,P(B).
$$ {#eq-independence}
:::

This product form is the definition we use, because it is symmetric in $A$ and $B$ and needs
no positivity assumption. But its *meaning* is the "learning nothing" statement, which we now
show is equivalent.

::: {#thm-indep-equiv}
**Theorem (independence = unchanged conditional).** If $P(B) > 0$, then $A$ and $B$ are
independent (in the sense of @eq-independence) **if and only if** $P(A\mid B) = P(A)$.
:::

::: {.proof}
We prove both directions.

*($\Rightarrow$)* Suppose $P(A\cap B) = P(A)P(B)$. Divide both sides by $P(B)>0$:
$$
\frac{P(A\cap B)}{P(B)} = \frac{P(A)P(B)}{P(B)} = P(A).
$$
The left side is $P(A\mid B)$ by definition @eq-conditional, so $P(A\mid B)=P(A)$.

*($\Leftarrow$)* Suppose $P(A\mid B) = P(A)$. By definition $P(A\mid B) = P(A\cap B)/P(B)$, so
$P(A\cap B)/P(B) = P(A)$. Multiply both sides by $P(B)$: $P(A\cap B) = P(A)P(B)$, which is
@eq-independence. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

So @eq-independence and "$P(A\mid B)=P(A)$" say the same thing: **the fraction of $B$ taken
up by $A$ is the same as the fraction of all of $\Omega$ taken up by $A$** — zooming into $B$
did not change $A$'s share. By the symmetric multiplication rule the same algebra gives
$P(B\mid A)=P(B)$ when $P(A)>0$: independence is mutual, as it must be.

### The classic confusion: independent is *not* mutually exclusive {#sec-disjoint-indep}

This is the single most-tested point in the topic, and the source of endless error. **Mutually
exclusive** (disjoint, from [PROB-00]) means $A\cap B = \varnothing$: $A$ and $B$ *cannot both
happen*. **Independent** means $P(A\cap B) = P(A)P(B)$: knowing one happened does not change
the other's probability. These are not the same — in fact, for events of positive probability
they are nearly *opposite*.

::: {#thm-disjoint-dependent}
**Theorem.** If $A$ and $B$ are mutually exclusive ($A\cap B=\varnothing$) and both have
positive probability ($P(A)>0$ and $P(B)>0$), then they are **not** independent — in fact
they are maximally dependent.
:::

::: {.proof}
Since $A\cap B=\varnothing$, we have $P(A\cap B) = P(\varnothing) = 0$ ($P(\varnothing)=0$ was
derived in [PROB-00]). For independence we would need $P(A\cap B)=P(A)P(B)$, i.e.
$0 = P(A)P(B)$. But $P(A)>0$ and $P(B)>0$ make the product strictly positive,
$P(A)P(B) > 0 \ne 0$. So the independence equation fails, and the events are dependent.

Concretely, $P(A\mid B) = P(A\cap B)/P(B) = 0/P(B) = 0$, whereas $P(A) > 0$. Learning $B$
occurred drove $A$'s probability all the way to $0$ — the *opposite* of leaving it unchanged.
That is why disjoint, positive-probability events are maximally *dependent*. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The intuition resolves the confusion completely: if $A$ and $B$ can never co-occur, then
learning $B$ happened is the strongest possible evidence *against* $A$ — it rules $A$ out
entirely. That is the very definition of dependence. The two ideas feel similar only because
both involve a pair of events and the word "and"; mathematically they pull in opposite
directions. @fig-disjoint-indep shows the two pictures side by side.

```{python}
#| label: fig-disjoint-indep
#| fig-cap: 'Mutually exclusive versus independent — opposite pictures. **Left (mutually exclusive):** $A$ and $B$ do not touch, so $P(A\\cap B)=0$. Learning $B$ rules $A$ out: $P(A\\mid B)=0\\ne P(A)$ — maximal *dependence*. **Right (independent):** $A$ and $B$ overlap by exactly $P(A)P(B)$; here $P(A)=0.5$ and $P(B)=0.4$ are realized as a left strip and a bottom strip of the unit square, and their overlap (purple) is the rectangle of area $0.5\\times0.4=0.2=P(A)P(B)$. The fraction of $B$ that lies in $A$ equals the fraction of all of $\\Omega$ that lies in $A$ — learning $B$ changes nothing.'
from matplotlib.patches import Rectangle, Circle
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 4.4))

# Left: mutually exclusive
axL.add_patch(Rectangle((0, 0), 1, 1, fill=False, edgecolor="black", lw=1.5))
axL.add_patch(Circle((0.30, 0.55), 0.20, color=SETA, alpha=0.55))
axL.add_patch(Circle((0.72, 0.40), 0.20, color=SETB, alpha=0.55))
axL.text(0.30, 0.55, "$A$", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=13)
axL.text(0.72, 0.40, "$B$", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=13)
axL.set_xlim(-0.05, 1.05); axL.set_ylim(-0.05, 1.18)
axL.set_aspect("equal"); axL.axis("off")
axL.set_title("Mutually exclusive:\n$P(A\\cap B)=0$, dependent", fontsize=11)
axL.text(0.5, 1.07, r"$\Omega$", ha="center", fontsize=12)

# Right: independent as orthogonal strips
axR.add_patch(Rectangle((0, 0), 1, 1, fill=False, edgecolor="black", lw=1.5))
axR.add_patch(Rectangle((0, 0), 0.5, 1, color=SETA, alpha=0.35))   # A: left strip
axR.add_patch(Rectangle((0, 0), 1, 0.4, color=SETB, alpha=0.35))   # B: bottom strip
axR.add_patch(Rectangle((0, 0), 0.5, 0.4, color="#7b4fa0", alpha=0.55))  # overlap
axR.text(0.25, 0.85, "$A$ ($0.5$)", ha="center", fontsize=11, color=SETA)
axR.text(0.80, 0.20, "$B$ ($0.4$)", ha="center", fontsize=11, color=SETB)
axR.text(0.25, 0.20, r"$A\cap B$" + "\n$0.2$", ha="center", va="center",
         fontsize=9, color="white")
axR.set_xlim(-0.05, 1.05); axR.set_ylim(-0.05, 1.18)
axR.set_aspect("equal"); axR.axis("off")
axR.set_title("Independent:\n$P(A\\cap B)=P(A)P(B)$", fontsize=11)
axR.text(0.5, 1.07, r"$\Omega$", ha="center", fontsize=12)

plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Are mutually exclusive events independent?"* No — almost never. If both have positive
probability, mutual exclusivity makes them *maximally dependent* (@thm-disjoint-dependent):
learning one occurred drives the other's probability to $0$. The only way disjoint events can
*also* be independent is the degenerate case where at least one has probability $0$ (then
both $P(A\cap B)$ and $P(A)P(B)$ are $0$ and the equation holds vacuously). If an interviewer
offers "they can't both happen, so they're independent," the trap is baited; the correct
answer is the reverse.
:::

### Independence of three or more events {#sec-mutual-indep}

For three events, pairwise independence is *not* enough — we need the product rule to hold for
every sub-collection. This subtlety trips people up, so we state it and give the standard
counterexample rather than waving it past.

::: {#def-mutual-independence}
**Definition (mutual independence).** Events $A_1, \dots, A_n$ are **mutually independent** if
for *every* sub-collection $\{A_{i_1}, \dots, A_{i_k}\}$ (with $2 \le k \le n$),
$$
P(A_{i_1}\cap\cdots\cap A_{i_k}) = P(A_{i_1})\cdots P(A_{i_k}).
$$
For three events $A,B,C$ this is four equations: the three pairwise products *and*
$P(A\cap B\cap C)=P(A)P(B)P(C)$.
:::

That the triple equation does not follow from the three pairwise ones is shown by a concrete
counterexample, worked in @sec-pairwise below. The takeaway you should carry into an exam:
**"pairwise independent" and "mutually independent" are different**, and i.i.d. samples
([PROB-08]) demand the full mutual version.

## The law of total probability {#sec-total}

To get to Bayes' rule we need one more tool: a way to compute the probability of an event by
splitting it across a set of mutually exclusive scenarios. This is the **law of total
probability**, and it is just the multiplication rule summed over a partition.

::: {#def-partition}
**Definition (partition).** A collection of events $B_1, B_2, \dots, B_n$ is a **partition**
of $\Omega$ if they are pairwise disjoint ($B_i\cap B_j=\varnothing$ for $i\ne j$) and their
union is everything ($\bigcup_i B_i = \Omega$). Exactly one of them occurs on every run — the
$B_i$ are an exhaustive list of mutually exclusive scenarios.
:::

::: {#thm-total}
**Theorem (law of total probability).** Let $B_1, \dots, B_n$ be a partition of $\Omega$ with
each $P(B_i) > 0$. Then for any event $A$,
$$
P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A\mid B_i)\,P(B_i).
$$ {#eq-total}
:::

::: {.proof}
The idea: slice $A$ into the pieces that fall in each scenario, add the pieces, then rewrite
each piece with the multiplication rule. Because the $B_i$ partition $\Omega$, we can carve
$A$ accordingly:
$$
A = A\cap\Omega = A\cap\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^n B_i\right) = \bigcup_{i=1}^n (A\cap B_i),
$$
where the last step is the distributive law of sets (intersecting $A$ with a union is the
union of the intersections; verified by outcome-chasing in [PROB-00]). These pieces
$A\cap B_1, \dots, A\cap B_n$ are pairwise disjoint, inherited from the $B_i$: if
$B_i\cap B_j=\varnothing$ then $(A\cap B_i)\cap(A\cap B_j)\subseteq B_i\cap B_j=\varnothing$.
So finite additivity ([PROB-00]) applies:
$$
P(A) = P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^n (A\cap B_i)\right) = \sum_{i=1}^n P(A\cap B_i).
$$
Now rewrite each term with the multiplication rule @eq-mult, $P(A\cap B_i)=P(A\mid B_i)P(B_i)$
(valid since $P(B_i)>0$):
$$
P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^n P(A\mid B_i)\,P(B_i),
$$
which is @eq-total. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

In words: to find the overall probability of $A$, ask "how likely is $A$ *within* each
scenario?" ($P(A\mid B_i)$), weight each by how likely that scenario is ($P(B_i)$), and add.
The simplest useful case is the two-scenario partition $\{B, B^c\}$ (an event and its
complement always partition $\Omega$, from [PROB-00]):
$$
P(A) = P(A\mid B)\,P(B) + P(A\mid B^c)\,P(B^c).
$$ {#eq-total2}
This two-term version is the denominator of Bayes' rule in almost every application below.

## Bayes' rule — inverting the conditional {#sec-bayes}

Now the destination. We can usually measure $P(B\mid A)$ — the probability of the evidence
given the cause — but we want $P(A\mid B)$ — the probability of the cause given the evidence.
Bayes' rule flips one into the other, and it is nothing more than writing the multiplication
rule two ways and dividing.

::: {#thm-bayes}
**Theorem (Bayes' rule).** For events $A$ and $B$ with $P(A) > 0$ and $P(B) > 0$,
$$
P(A\mid B) = \frac{P(B\mid A)\,P(A)}{P(B)}.
$$ {#eq-bayes}
If $B_1, \dots, B_n$ partition $\Omega$, then for each $j$,
$$
P(B_j\mid A) = \frac{P(A\mid B_j)\,P(B_j)}{\sum_{i=1}^n P(A\mid B_i)\,P(B_i)}.
$$ {#eq-bayes-partition}
:::

::: {.proof}
The intersection $A\cap B$ is one quantity, and the multiplication rule @eq-mult expresses it
in *two* ways — conditioning on $B$, or conditioning on $A$:
$$
P(A\cap B) = P(A\mid B)\,P(B) \qquad\text{and}\qquad P(A\cap B) = P(B\mid A)\,P(A).
$$
Both equal the same $P(A\cap B)$, so they equal each other:
$$
P(A\mid B)\,P(B) = P(B\mid A)\,P(A).
$$
Divide both sides by $P(B)$ (legitimate, $P(B)>0$):
$$
P(A\mid B) = \frac{P(B\mid A)\,P(A)}{P(B)},
$$
which is @eq-bayes. That is the entire derivation — Bayes' rule is the multiplication rule,
read from both ends and divided.

For the partitioned form @eq-bayes-partition, apply @eq-bayes with $B_j$ in the role of $A$
and the event $A$ in the role of $B$:
$$
P(B_j\mid A) = \frac{P(A\mid B_j)\,P(B_j)}{P(A)}.
$$
Then expand the denominator $P(A)$ by the law of total probability @eq-total over the
partition $\{B_i\}$, giving the stated formula. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The names of the four pieces of @eq-bayes are worth fixing, because the whole Bayesian
vocabulary is built on them. Think of $A$ as a *hypothesis* (a cause) and $B$ as the
*evidence* we observed:

- $P(A)$ is the **prior** — the probability of the hypothesis *before* seeing the evidence.
- $P(B\mid A)$ is the **likelihood** — how probable the evidence is *if* the hypothesis holds.
- $P(B)$ is the **marginal likelihood** or **evidence** — the overall probability of the
  observation, typically computed by total probability @eq-total2 across all hypotheses.
- $P(A\mid B)$ is the **posterior** — the updated probability of the hypothesis *after*
  folding in the evidence.

So Bayes' rule reads $\text{posterior} = \dfrac{\text{likelihood}\times\text{prior}}{\text{evidence}}$.
The prior is the term that is forgotten most often, and forgetting it is exactly the error in
the worked example we turn to next.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What's the difference between $P(A\mid B)$ and $P(B\mid A)$ — aren't they close?"* They can
be wildly different, and conflating them is the **prosecutor's fallacy** (@sec-prosecutor).
$P(\text{evidence}\mid\text{innocent})$ being tiny does *not* make
$P(\text{innocent}\mid\text{evidence})$ tiny — the two are related by Bayes' rule and differ
by the ratio of priors $P(A)/P(B)$, which can be enormous when the cause is rare. The
interviewer wants to see that you reach for @eq-bayes to convert one into the other, and that
you never assume they are equal.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We run the machinery on four genuine problems: the rare-disease test (where the base rate
ambushes everyone), a spam filter (where independence is *assumed though false*, on purpose —
the edge of applicability), a courtroom (the prosecutor's fallacy), and the
pairwise-but-not-mutually-independent counterexample promised above.

## Example 1 — the rare-disease test, where the base rate ambushes you {#sec-disease}

A disease affects $1$ in $1000$ people, so the prior is $P(D) = 0.001$. A test is "$99\%$
accurate" in the usual sense: it correctly flags $99\%$ of sick people (the **sensitivity**,
$P(+\mid D) = 0.99$) and correctly clears $99\%$ of healthy people (the **specificity**,
$P(-\mid D^c) = 0.99$, so the false-positive rate is $P(+\mid D^c) = 0.01$). A patient tests
positive. **What is the probability they actually have the disease?**

The instinct — and the answer most people, including many clinicians, blurt out — is "about
$99\%$." That confuses $P(+\mid D)$ with $P(D\mid +)$. Bayes' rule, with the denominator
expanded by total probability @eq-total2 over the partition $\{D, D^c\}$:
$$
P(D\mid +) = \frac{P(+\mid D)\,P(D)}{P(+\mid D)\,P(D) + P(+\mid D^c)\,P(D^c)}.
$$
Plug in $P(D)=0.001$, $P(D^c)=0.999$, $P(+\mid D)=0.99$, $P(+\mid D^c)=0.01$:

```{python}
#| label: tbl-disease
#| echo: true
P_D     = 0.001        # prior: disease prevalence
P_Dc    = 1 - P_D
sens    = 0.99         # P(+ | D)   sensitivity
fpr     = 0.01         # P(+ | D^c) false-positive rate

# denominator: total probability of a positive test
P_pos = sens * P_D + fpr * P_Dc
P_D_given_pos = (sens * P_D) / P_pos

print(f"P(+)         = {sens}*{P_D} + {fpr}*{P_Dc} = {P_pos:.5f}")
print(f"P(D | +)     = {sens*P_D:.5f} / {P_pos:.5f} = {P_D_given_pos:.4f}")
print(f"           => about {100*P_D_given_pos:.1f}% chance of disease given a positive test")
print(f"For comparison, the test's sensitivity P(+|D) = {sens} = {100*sens:.0f}%")
```

The answer is roughly **$9\%$**, not $99\%$. Despite a "$99\%$ accurate" test, a positive
result means the patient *probably does not* have the disease. The reason is the **base
rate**: the disease is so rare ($0.1\%$) that the small false-positive rate ($1\%$) acting on
the huge healthy population ($99.9\%$) produces *more* false positives than there are true
positives in the tiny sick population. Concretely, per $100{,}000$ people: about $100$ are
sick and $99$ test positive (true positives); about $99{,}900$ are healthy and $\approx 999$
test positive (false positives). Of the $\approx 1098$ positives, only $99$ are real —
$99/1098 \approx 9\%$. @fig-disease makes this population picture concrete and shows how the
answer moves as the disease becomes less rare.

```{python}
#| label: fig-disease
#| fig-cap: 'Why the base rate dominates. **Left:** a population of $100{,}000$ split into true positives (sick *and* positive, green) versus false positives (healthy *but* positive, red). False positives swamp true positives because the healthy pool is enormous — so most positive tests are wrong. **Right:** the posterior $P(D\\mid+)$ as a function of disease prevalence $P(D)$, holding the test at $99\\%$ sensitivity and specificity. At the $0.001$ prevalence (dashed) the posterior is only $\\approx 0.09$; the test becomes trustworthy only once the disease is common. This is the honest reason rare-disease screening needs confirmatory testing.'
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.4, 4.2))

# Left: population bar of positives
N = 100_000
sick = N * P_D
healthy = N * P_Dc
true_pos = sick * sens
false_pos = healthy * fpr
axL.bar(["true positives\n(sick & +)", "false positives\n(healthy & +)"],
        [true_pos, false_pos], color=[ACCENT, LINE], alpha=0.85)
axL.set_ylabel("count among 100,000 tested")
axL.set_title("Among all positive tests")
for i, v in enumerate([true_pos, false_pos]):
    axL.text(i, v + 15, f"{v:.0f}", ha="center", fontsize=10)

# Right: posterior vs prevalence
prev = np.logspace(-4, 0, 400)
post = (sens * prev) / (sens * prev + fpr * (1 - prev))
axR.plot(prev, post, color=POINT)
axR.axvline(P_D, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.3)
axR.axhline(P_D_given_pos, color=LINE, ls=":", lw=1.3)
axR.scatter([P_D], [P_D_given_pos], color=LINE, zorder=5)
axR.annotate(f"prevalence {P_D},\nposterior {P_D_given_pos:.2f}",
             xy=(P_D, P_D_given_pos), xytext=(0.004, 0.55), fontsize=9,
             arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=GREY))
axR.set_xscale("log")
axR.set_xlabel("disease prevalence $P(D)$ (log scale)")
axR.set_ylabel("posterior $P(D \\mid +)$")
axR.set_title("Posterior depends on the base rate")
axR.set_ylim(0, 1)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall — the base-rate fallacy"}
The mistake is reading "$99\%$ accurate" as "$P(\text{disease}\mid\text{positive})=0.99$." It
is not; that figure is the *sensitivity* $P(\text{positive}\mid\text{disease})$, the
*backward* conditional. The posterior also depends on the **prior** $P(D)$, and when the
prior is tiny it dominates the answer. This is the base-rate fallacy, and it is why a single
positive screening test for a rare condition is followed by a confirmatory test rather than
treatment. Bayes' rule does not let you skip the base rate; the term $P(A)$ in @eq-bayes is
not optional.
:::

## Example 2 — a spam filter, and the edge of applicability {#sec-spam}

Now an example deliberately chosen at the **edge of applicability**, where the clean theory
is applied to data that *violates its assumption* — and works anyway, for reasons worth being
honest about. A naive-Bayes spam filter classifies an email by the words it contains. Suppose
across a training corpus we measured, for a small vocabulary of three trigger words:

| word $w$    | $P(w\mid \text{spam})$ | $P(w\mid \text{ham})$ |
|-------------|------------------------:|----------------------:|
| *free*      | 0.40 | 0.04 |
| *invoice*   | 0.20 | 0.10 |
| *meeting*   | 0.02 | 0.30 |

with a prior $P(\text{spam}) = 0.30$ (so $P(\text{ham}) = 0.70$). An email arrives containing
*free* and *invoice* but **not** *meeting*. What is $P(\text{spam}\mid\text{this email})$?

The honest difficulty: to apply Bayes' rule we need the *joint* likelihood of the whole word
pattern, $P(\text{free},\,\text{invoice},\,\overline{\text{meeting}}\mid\text{spam})$. Words
in real email are emphatically **not** independent — "invoice" and "meeting" co-occur in
legitimate business mail far more than chance would predict. But modeling the full joint
distribution of all word combinations is intractable, so the **naive** assumption is made *on
purpose*: pretend the words are conditionally independent **given the class**. Under that
assumption the joint likelihood factors into a product (this is exactly mutual independence,
@def-mutual-independence, applied conditionally):
$$
P(w_1, w_2, \overline{w_3}\mid C)
= P(w_1\mid C)\,P(w_2\mid C)\,\bigl(1 - P(w_3\mid C)\bigr),
$$
for class $C\in\{\text{spam},\text{ham}\}$, where $\overline{w_3}$ means *meeting* is absent
so its contribution is $1 - P(\text{meeting}\mid C)$. Then Bayes' rule @eq-bayes-partition
gives the posterior.

```{python}
#| label: fig-spam
#| fig-cap: 'Naive-Bayes spam scoring under a deliberately false independence assumption. **Left:** the per-class likelihood contributions of the observed pattern (*free*, *invoice*, no *meeting*), multiplied under conditional independence, times the prior, give an unnormalized score for each class. **Right:** normalizing those two scores yields the posterior $P(\\text{spam}\\mid\\text{email})\\approx 0.92$. The independence assumption is *wrong* — words correlate — yet the *ranking* of the two classes is usually right, which is all a classifier needs. The calibrated probability, however, is not trustworthy; that is the edge where the method strains.'
words_obs = {"free": True, "invoice": True, "meeting": False}
lik = {
    "free":    {"spam": 0.40, "ham": 0.04},
    "invoice": {"spam": 0.20, "ham": 0.10},
    "meeting": {"spam": 0.02, "ham": 0.30},
}
prior = {"spam": 0.30, "ham": 0.70}

def class_score(c):
    s = prior[c]
    for w, present in words_obs.items():
        p = lik[w][c]
        s *= p if present else (1 - p)
    return s

score = {c: class_score(c) for c in ("spam", "ham")}
Z = score["spam"] + score["ham"]
post_spam = score["spam"] / Z

print("unnormalized scores (likelihood x prior):")
for c in ("spam", "ham"):
    print(f"   {c:4s}: {score[c]:.6f}")
print(f"P(spam | email) = {score['spam']:.6f} / {Z:.6f} = {post_spam:.4f}")

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 4.0))
axL.bar(["spam", "ham"], [score["spam"], score["ham"]],
        color=[LINE, ACCENT], alpha=0.85)
axL.set_ylabel("unnormalized score")
axL.set_title("likelihood $\\times$ prior, per class")
axR.bar(["spam", "ham"], [post_spam, 1 - post_spam],
        color=[LINE, ACCENT], alpha=0.85)
axR.set_ylabel("posterior probability")
axR.set_ylim(0, 1)
axR.set_title(f"normalized: $P(\\mathrm{{spam}}\\mid\\mathrm{{email}})={post_spam:.2f}$")
plt.tight_layout()
```

The filter is highly confident the email is spam. Now the honest accounting of *where this
strains*: the independence assumption is false, so the *number* $0.92$ is not a calibrated
probability — when words are positively correlated, multiplying their likelihoods
double-counts shared evidence and pushes the posterior toward $0$ or $1$ more aggressively
than the truth warrants. What survives the bad assumption is the **ordering**: spam still
scores above ham, so the *classification* is usually correct even though the *probability* is
overconfident. That is the precise edge of applicability — naive Bayes is a good *classifier*
and a poor *probability estimator*, and you should reach for a model that respects the
dependence (logistic regression, or a model with correlated features) when you need
calibrated probabilities rather than just a label.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall — assuming independence to make the math go"}
Independence turns an intractable joint probability into a tidy product, which is why it is
assumed so often. But it is an *assumption about the world*, not a mathematical identity, and
it is usually at least slightly false. The danger is forgetting you made it: the resulting
probabilities can be badly miscalibrated even when the classifier's decisions are fine. Always
name the independence assumption you are leaning on, and ask whether the quantity you report
(a label, or a calibrated probability) actually survives it.
:::

## Example 3 — the prosecutor's fallacy {#sec-prosecutor}

A forensic match (DNA, fiber, a rare blood type) is found linking a defendant to a crime
scene. The expert testifies that the chance of such a match *if the defendant were innocent*
is one in a million: $P(\text{match}\mid\text{innocent}) = 10^{-6}$. The prosecutor concludes
the defendant is almost certainly guilty — "only a one-in-a-million chance of innocence." This
is the **prosecutor's fallacy**, and it is the courtroom face of confusing $P(B\mid A)$ with
$P(A\mid B)$.

What the jury wants is $P(\text{innocent}\mid\text{match})$, not
$P(\text{match}\mid\text{innocent})$. Bayes' rule relates them, and the missing ingredient is
the **prior** — how many people *could* have been the source. Suppose the relevant population
is a city of $1{,}000{,}000$ potential suspects, so before any evidence the prior on this
particular person being the culprit is $P(\text{guilty}) = 1/1{,}000{,}000$, and
$P(\text{innocent}) = 1 - 10^{-6}$. Assume a true culprit certainly matches,
$P(\text{match}\mid\text{guilty}) = 1$.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-prosecutor
#| echo: true
P_guilty  = 1 / 1_000_000        # prior: one specific person among a million
P_innoc   = 1 - P_guilty
P_match_g = 1.0                  # the true culprit matches
P_match_i = 1e-6                 # random-match probability for an innocent person

# total probability of a match
P_match = P_match_g * P_guilty + P_match_i * P_innoc
P_innoc_given_match = (P_match_i * P_innoc) / P_match
P_guilty_given_match = (P_match_g * P_guilty) / P_match

print(f"Expert's figure  P(match | innocent) = {P_match_i:.1e}")
print(f"What the jury needs:")
print(f"   P(guilty  | match) = {P_guilty_given_match:.4f}  ({100*P_guilty_given_match:.0f}%)")
print(f"   P(innocent| match) = {P_innoc_given_match:.4f}  ({100*P_innoc_given_match:.0f}%)")
```

The probability of innocence given the match is about **$50\%$**, not one in a million. The
reason mirrors the disease example exactly: in a city of a million innocent people, a
one-in-a-million random-match rate produces, on average, about *one* innocent match — roughly
as many as the single guilty match. The match alone is a coin flip, not proof. The expert's
number $P(\text{match}\mid\text{innocent}) = 10^{-6}$ was correct; the prosecutor's
*interpretation* of it as $P(\text{innocent}\mid\text{match})$ was the fallacy, and Bayes'
rule is what exposes the gap — the two conditionals differ by the ratio of priors, which here
is a factor of about a million.

## Example 4 — pairwise independent but not mutually independent {#sec-pairwise}

We promised (@sec-mutual-indep) a case where every pair is independent yet the three together
are not. Take two independent fair coin flips and define three events:
$$
A = \{\text{first flip is heads}\},\quad
B = \{\text{second flip is heads}\},\quad
C = \{\text{the two flips agree}\}.
$$

```{python}
#| label: fig-pairwise
#| fig-cap: 'Pairwise independence does not imply mutual independence. Over the four equally likely outcomes of two fair coins, the events $A$ (first heads), $B$ (second heads), and $C$ (flips agree) are *pairwise* independent — each pairwise product matches — yet $P(A\\cap B\\cap C)=1/4\\ne P(A)P(B)P(C)=1/8$. Knowing any two of $A,B,C$ pins down the third exactly, so the triple is dependent even though no pair is. The bars compare the joint probability against the product of marginals for each collection; equal heights mean independence, unequal means dependence.'
import itertools
omega = list(itertools.product([0, 1], repeat=2))   # (flip1, flip2), 1=heads
N = len(omega)
A = [o for o in omega if o[0] == 1]
B = [o for o in omega if o[1] == 1]
C = [o for o in omega if o[0] == o[1]]
def P(ev): return len(ev) / N
def inter(*evs):
    s = set(omega)
    for e in evs: s &= set(e)
    return list(s)

pairs = [("A,B", inter(A, B), P(A)*P(B)),
         ("A,C", inter(A, C), P(A)*P(C)),
         ("B,C", inter(B, C), P(B)*P(C)),
         ("A,B,C", inter(A, B, C), P(A)*P(B)*P(C))]
print(f"P(A)={P(A)}, P(B)={P(B)}, P(C)={P(C)}")
for name, joint, prod in pairs:
    tag = "independent" if abs(P(joint)-prod) < 1e-12 else "DEPENDENT"
    print(f"  {name:6s}: P(joint)={P(joint):.3f}  product={prod:.3f}  -> {tag}")

labels = [p[0] for p in pairs]
joints = [P(p[1]) for p in pairs]
prods  = [p[2] for p in pairs]
x = np.arange(len(labels)); w = 0.38
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.0))
ax.bar(x - w/2, joints, w, label="$P(\\mathrm{joint})$", color=POINT, alpha=0.85)
ax.bar(x + w/2, prods,  w, label="product of marginals", color=SETB, alpha=0.85)
ax.set_xticks(x); ax.set_xticklabels(labels)
ax.set_ylabel("probability")
ax.set_title("Pairwise independent, jointly dependent")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
```

Each event has probability $1/2$. Every *pair* is independent: for instance
$P(A\cap C) = P(\text{first heads and flips agree}) = P(\text{HH}) = 1/4 = P(A)P(C)$, and
likewise for $A,B$ and $B,C$. But the triple fails:
$P(A\cap B\cap C) = P(\text{HH}) = 1/4$, while $P(A)P(B)P(C) = (1/2)^3 = 1/8$. They are not
equal, so $A,B,C$ are **not** mutually independent — and indeed they cannot be, because *any
two of them determine the third* (if the first is heads and they agree, the second must be
heads). This is exactly why @def-mutual-independence demands the product rule for *every*
sub-collection, the full equation set, not just the pairs.

# When to use this — and when not to {#sec-applicability}

Conditioning and Bayes' rule are nearly universal, so the applicability question is less
"does it apply?" than "are its inputs trustworthy?" The honest boundaries:

- **Use Bayes' rule** whenever you can measure a conditional in the *forward* direction
  (evidence given cause: test accuracy, match rates, word frequencies) but need it in the
  *backward* direction (cause given evidence). This is the overwhelming majority of diagnostic,
  forensic, and classification problems. It is exact and assumption-free *given* its inputs.
- **The catch is always the prior and the likelihood.** Bayes' rule is a flawless converter of
  probabilities, but it inherits every flaw in what you feed it. A wrong base rate (the disease
  example) or an indefensible prior (the courtroom population) produces a confident wrong
  answer. When the prior is genuinely unknown and contested, the posterior is too — and that is
  a feature, not a bug, but it means Bayes does not manufacture certainty from nothing.
- **Independence is the assumption to audit.** Computing joint probabilities by multiplying
  marginals (the spam filter, i.i.d. samples in [PROB-08]) requires independence that is usually
  at best approximate. Use it when the dependence is weak or when you only need a *ranking*
  (classification); reach for a dependence-aware model (joint distributions in [PROB-07],
  conditional distributions in [PROB-09], logistic regression downstream) when you need
  calibrated joint probabilities.
- **When conditioning is the wrong frame entirely.** If you actually want a *causal* effect —
  "what happens to $Y$ if I *intervene* and set $X$?" — conditioning $P(Y\mid X)$ is **not** the
  answer; that is the domain of the Causal Inference track, where conditioning on observed data
  and intervening are formally different operations. Bayes' rule updates beliefs from
  observation; it does not, by itself, license causal claims.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Where would conditional probability actually be used in practice, and where would it mislead
you?"* It is the backbone of medical diagnosis, spam/fraud detection, A/B-test interpretation,
recommendation systems, and any Bayesian model. It misleads when the base rate is ignored
(@sec-disease), when independence is assumed and the features correlate (@sec-spam), when
forward and backward conditionals are swapped (@sec-prosecutor), or when an observational
conditional is mistaken for a causal effect. The graduate-level answer names both the ubiquity
*and* these four failure modes, and reaches for Bayes' rule to make the update explicit rather
than reasoning by gut.
:::

# How you would actually compute with this {#sec-implementation}

The math is primary; this is the secondary, practical note. Every computation above is a few
lines of arithmetic — conditional probability is `P(A_and_B) / P(B)`, Bayes' rule is
`likelihood * prior / evidence`, and the evidence is a sum over a partition (the law of total
probability). The one numerical caution worth stating: when you chain many likelihoods (the
spam filter with thousands of words, or any sequence model), the product of many small
probabilities **underflows** to zero in floating point. The standard fix is to work in **log
space** — replace the product $\prod_i P(w_i\mid C)$ with the sum $\sum_i \log P(w_i\mid C)$,
add the log-prior, and compare classes by their log-scores, exponentiating only at the end (or
using a numerically stable normalization). The mathematics is identical because $\log$ is
monotone, so the *ranking* of classes is preserved; this is purely a floating-point safeguard,
and it is how every real naive-Bayes and likelihood computation is implemented. The libraries
you will reach for (`scipy.stats`, scikit-learn's `naive_bayes`) do exactly this internally;
knowing the derivation is knowing what they compute and why.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
This article sits one step above the ground floor, and conditioning is the hinge the rest of
the track swings on:

- **It came from [PROB-00 *What Is Probability?*]** — conditioning is *restriction of the
  sample space* plus *renormalization*, and we proved the conditional is itself a probability
  on the restricted world using the very axioms [PROB-00] established. It also settles the
  disjoint-versus-independent confusion [PROB-00] flagged.
- **[PROB-08 *Independence and the i.i.d. Sample*]** lifts the event-independence of @sec-independence
  to *random variables* and to the i.i.d. assumption every downstream track leans on; the
  pairwise-versus-mutual subtlety (@sec-pairwise) becomes the precise content of "i.i.d."
- **[PROB-09 *Conditional Distributions and Conditional Expectation*]** rebuilds conditioning
  at the random-variable level — $f(y\mid x)$ and $E[Y\mid X]$ — including the delicate case
  $P(B)=0$ we set aside in @def-conditional, and is where Bayes' rule becomes Bayesian
  *updating of distributions*.
- **[PROB-12 *The Poisson Distribution*]** uses independence of events directly in the
  law-of-rare-events construction.

Cross-track, this is the article that **naive-Bayes classification** (Classification track),
**Bayesian inference** (Inference E4 — note: Bayes *for parameters*, posterior $\propto$
likelihood $\times$ prior, lives there; Bayes *for events* lives here), and **causal
reasoning** all point back to. The applicability note above marks the seam where conditioning
ends and causation begins.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The founding source.** T. Bayes, "An Essay towards Solving a Problem in the Doctrine of
  Chances," *Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society* (1763), communicated
  posthumously by Richard Price. Short, and the inversion problem is stated in its original
  geometric form (a ball rolled on a table).
- **The generalizer.** P.-S. Laplace, *Mémoire sur la probabilité des causes par les
  événements* (1774) and the later *Théorie analytique des probabilités* (1812), where Bayes'
  rule becomes a general method for reasoning from effects to causes — the true origin of the
  modern formula.
- **The base-rate fallacy, documented.** A. Tversky and D. Kahneman, "Evidential impact of
  base rates" (1982), and the classic finding that even trained clinicians badly misjudge
  $P(\text{disease}\mid\text{positive})$ — the cognitive shadow of @sec-disease.
- **The courtroom failure mode.** The literature on the *prosecutor's fallacy* (e.g. the
  analyses of *R v. Sally Clark*) shows @sec-prosecutor playing out with real consequences;
  worth reading for how a swapped conditional convicts the innocent.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *Conditioning on a probability-zero
  event* (continuous $B$) — handled by densities in [PROB-09]. *Independence of random
  variables and the i.i.d. assumption* — [PROB-08]. *Bayesian updating of an entire
  distribution* rather than a single event — Inference E4. *When conditioning is not the same
  as intervening* — the Causal Inference track.
